summary: DeVault describes his ideal note-taking application: an infinite canvas where pages, flowcharts, tables, drawings, and other objects can be placed freely. He outlines specific requirements including multi-input support (keyboard, trackpad, drawing tablet), high performance with real-time rasterization, and export capabilities. He acknowledges he lacks bandwidth to build it himself and hopes to inspire others. The tool should be free software running on Linux, and he'd pay up to $1000 one-time or $100/month for collaboration features.
key_points: Physical notebooks work well for ad-hoc notes but fail at sharing, editing, straight lines, and typing speed
The ideal tool is an infinite grid canvas supporting pages, flowcharts, tables, images, and freehand drawing as first-class objects
Input should embrace every mode: mouse, keyboard, trackpad, touch, and drawing tablets with shape recognition
Performance is non-negotiable — zooming and panning must rasterize objects in real-time without stutter
He would pay premium prices ($1000 one-time or $100/month) for a free software tool that meets these requirements
money_quotes: I wish this was a digital medium, but there are not any applications available which can support the note-taking paradigm that I wish I could have.
The essential interface would be an infinite grid on which various kinds of objects can be placed by the user.
I'd love to be able to make flowcharts by drawing boxes and arrows and having the software recognize them and align them to the grid as first-class vector objects.

Performance is important here. I should be able to zoom in and out and pan around while all of the objects rasterize themselves in real-time, never making the user suffer through stuttery interactions.
Naturally this application should be free software and should run on Linux.

I would be willing to pay a premium price for this tool — a one-time fee of as much as $1000, or subscriptions on the order of $100/month if real-time collaboration or cloud synchronization are included.
key_insight: The gap between physical notebooks and existing digital tools remains wide, and a free software infinite canvas combining structured documents with freehand input could be worth serious money to power users.
